worth_it_explanation: A publisher's Google Ad Manager (GAM) numbers rarely match a partner exchange or supply-side platform's numbers exactly, so the question is never whether a gap exists but whether it sits inside the normal range. A reconciliation practice that compares the two reports for the same period and knows that range is what separates a billing error or a revenue leak from expected counting noise. The check is reporting work the auditor can do without platform access, and a gap that has gone untraced for months is where unbilled or under-collected revenue hides.
intro: Google Ad Manager and the exchanges, supply-side platforms, and direct partners a publisher works with count impressions at different moments and filter invalid traffic differently, so their reports for the same inventory rarely agree to the impression. Reconciliation is the practice of comparing the two reports for a shared period, knowing the tolerance that counts as normal, and tracing the gaps that fall outside it. These checks confirm that a reconciliation routine exists and that recurring discrepancies are attributed to a known cause rather than left as unexplained noise.

audit_checks.title: Ad Manager and partner reports are not reconciled
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A publisher whose Google Ad Manager and partner reports are never compared cannot tell a normal counting gap from a billing error or a revenue leak. A discrepancy that grows over a quarter, or a partner that consistently reports fewer paid impressions than Ad Manager served, goes unnoticed until the revenue shortfall is large enough to surface on its own.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) and a partner supply-side platform (SSP) or exchange count the same inventory from two different vantage points, so their reports rarely match to the impression. GAM counts an impression when a creative is downloaded and begins to load on the device, while a partner may count when it wins the bid, when the ad renders, or at a viewability event entirely. Each side also filters invalid traffic on its own schedule. The result is a gap that is expected, not a defect.

Reconciliation is the routine of pulling both reports for the same date range, comparing impressions and revenue, and recording the size of the gap. Google notes that discrepancies of up to 20 percent are normal for third-party creatives, and that a single-digit to low-double-digit percent gap is a typical range to treat as ordinary counting noise. A gap that sits inside that range and stays stable is the signal that both sides are healthy.

Without a reconciliation routine, the publisher has no baseline. A partner that begins reporting fewer paid impressions than GAM served, a gap that widens month over month, or a sudden jump after a tag change all read the same as silence. Each of those patterns can mean lost revenue or a billing error, and each is invisible until someone compares the two numbers on purpose.

A few signs point to reconciliation being absent:
  • No one can state the normal discrepancy range for a given partner
  • Partner revenue is accepted as paid without a check against what GAM recorded
  • A discrepancy is only investigated after a partner raises it, never before
You may want to confirm that someone owns the comparison on a regular cadence, for example monthly at billing time, rather than treating each report as correct in isolation. Google's guidance on investigating report discrepancies is the reference for what counts as a normal gap.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media property running several demand partners through GAM, each partner has its own normal discrepancy range, so a single tolerance across all of them hides the outlier. You may want to reconcile per partner rather than in aggregate, so a 25 percent gap on one exchange does not get averaged away by tighter gaps on the others.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content property where a share of revenue comes from a retail media or affiliate partner alongside display demand, the partner's report may count a different event than an impression, for example a click or a qualified visit. You may want to confirm you are comparing like units before reading the gap, rather than comparing GAM impressions against a partner's click or session count.

investigate.intro: Confirm that a reconciliation routine exists, then pull the same date range from Google Ad Manager and the partner, compare impressions and revenue, and record the gap against a known tolerance.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Reporting', then 'Reports', and build or open a report for a single partner's inventory covering a defined period, for example the prior calendar month.
Add the impressions and revenue columns and the dimension that isolates the partner, for example the yield group or the ad source, and note the totals for the period.

Read Google's guidance on investigating report discrepancies and note the counting point Ad Manager uses, which is the moment the creative begins to load, so you know what the partner's number is being compared against.

partner_report.label: Partner report
steps: Open the partner SSP or exchange's reporting for the same date range, the same inventory, and the same time zone, and record its paid impressions and revenue.
Calculate the gap as a percentage of the larger number for both impressions and revenue, and note whether it falls inside the range you treat as normal for that partner.
Compare the gap against prior periods if any record exists, so a stable gap can be told apart from one that is widening.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per partner, whether the two reports were pulled for a matched period, the impression and revenue gap as a percentage, and the tolerance treated as normal for that partner. A partner whose reports are reconciled on a regular cadence with the gap inside a known tolerance passes. A partner whose reports are never compared, or whose gap sits outside a known tolerance with no explanation, is a fail recorded with the partner name and the observed gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the matched-period gap and the known tolerance for each partner above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s Google Ad Manager (GAM) reports are not regularly reconciled against its demand partners' reports. GAM and a partner exchange count impressions at different points and filter invalid traffic differently, so a gap is expected, but without a reconciliation routine there is no baseline that separates a normal gap from a billing error or a revenue leak. {{voice_recommend}} establishing a regular reconciliation, for example monthly at billing time, that pulls both reports for the same period, time zone, and inventory, records the impression and revenue gap per partner, and flags any gap that falls outside the range that partner normally sits in. After the routine is in place, an out-of-tolerance gap surfaces as a question to trace rather than as a shortfall noticed after the fact.

audit_checks.title: The causes of recurring discrepancies are not traced
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A recurring discrepancy that is logged but never attributed to a cause leaves the publisher unable to tell expected noise from a real problem. A gap driven by a time-zone offset is harmless, while the same size gap driven by bidder timeouts or one-sided invalid-traffic filtering points to lost revenue, and without tracing the cause the two are indistinguishable.

educate.base:
A discrepancy that shows up every period has an identifiable cause, and naming that cause is what turns a number into a verdict. The same 12 percent gap can be harmless or a problem depending on what drives it, so the gap on its own does not tell the publisher whether to act.

Recurring discrepancies between Google Ad Manager (GAM) and a partner usually trace to one of a small set of causes:
  • Counting point: GAM counts when the creative begins to load, while a partner may count at the bid win or at the rendered ad, so the two measure different moments
  • Bidder timeouts: bids that arrive after the auction's timeout are dropped, so a partner records a response that GAM never counted as served
  • Invalid-traffic filtering: each side removes invalid traffic on its own schedule, so a filter applied on one side and not the other opens a gap
  • Time-zone offset: a report pulled in one time zone against a partner report in another shifts a slice of impressions into the wrong day
Each cause has a different meaning. A time-zone offset is a reporting artifact to correct in how the report is pulled, while bidder timeouts or one-sided filtering can mean impressions the publisher served are not being paid for. The counting-point gap is expected and stable, so a gap that grows is more likely one of the others.

The work is to attribute each recurring gap to its cause rather than accept the gap as a fixed cost. You may want to start by matching the date range and time zone exactly, which removes the offset cause, then read whether the remaining gap behaves like a counting difference or like dropped or filtered traffic. Google's notes on how impressions are counted and on the causes of cross-source discrepancies are the reference for which cause a pattern fits.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a property running header bidding or open bidding into GAM, bidder timeouts are a frequent driver of the gap. A partner that responds after the auction closes logs a bid GAM never served. You may want to check the timeout setting against the partner's typical response time, so a gap caused by an aggressive timeout is told apart from a counting difference.

learn_more.source_title: Google Analytics and Ad Manager discrepancies

investigate.intro: Take each recurring discrepancy and break it down by counting point, bidder timeout, invalid-traffic filtering, and time zone until the gap is attributed to a cause.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Reporting' and pull the partner's inventory for a period where the discrepancy recurs, confirming the report's time zone matches the partner report's time zone before reading any numbers.
Note GAM's counting point against the partner's, then check whether the remaining gap after the time zones are aligned matches the counting difference or exceeds it.

For inventory served through yield groups or open bidding, open the yield group or ad source settings and record the bidder timeout, so a gap that traces to dropped late bids can be separated from a counting difference.

partner_report.label: Partner report
steps: Pull the partner's report for the same aligned period and time zone, and note how the partner describes its counting point and its invalid-traffic filtering.
Compare the partner's invalid-traffic figure against GAM's. A filter applied on one side and not the other is a recurring cause of the gap.
Attribute the recurring gap to a single dominant cause where possible, for example a counting difference, a timeout, one-sided filtering, or a time-zone offset, and record it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per recurring discrepancy, whether the date range and time zone were aligned and which cause the remaining gap is attributed to. A recurring discrepancy that is matched on time zone and attributed to a known cause, for example a counting-point difference or a documented timeout, passes. A recurring discrepancy left unexplained, or one where a time-zone offset or one-sided filtering has not been ruled in or out, is a fail recorded with the partner name and the unresolved gap.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the aligned-period gap and the attributed cause for each recurring discrepancy above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} logs recurring discrepancies between Google Ad Manager (GAM) and its partners without tracing each one to a cause. A recurring gap can come from a different counting point, dropped late bids on a bidder timeout, invalid-traffic filtering applied on one side, or a time-zone offset between the two reports, and these mean different things. A counting difference is expected while dropped or filtered traffic can be unpaid revenue. {{voice_recommend}} attributing each recurring discrepancy to a cause by first aligning the date range and time zone, then reading whether the remaining gap behaves like a counting difference, a timeout, or one-sided filtering. With each gap named, an expected counting difference can be accepted and a revenue-affecting cause can be acted on.
